steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank completes a logical inference. The passage establishes that decarcinization often leads to extinction (an ‘evolutionary dead-end’), but then introduces frog crabs as a counterexample: a decarcinized group that has thrived in sediment since the Early Cretaceous. The blank must state what this counterexample suggests.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the evolutionary benefits of a crablike body form are less certain than many studies had implied': The passage does not cast doubt on carcinization in general. The evidence supports that carcinization is broadly beneficial; the counterexample only shows decarcinization can sometimes succeed—not that the crab form is questionable. ✗
  • Choice B — 'despite having many living relatives, some decarcinized groups did not benefit from decarcinization': This contradicts the passage. Frog crabs—the group under discussion—have few living relatives, not many, and they did benefit from decarcinization by thriving in sediment. ✗
  • Choice C — 'representation of practical applications of ecological knowledge is the defining characteristic of the music of certain Indigenous peoples': This is entirely unrelated to the passage about crustacean evolution. ✗
  • Choice D — 'a crablike body form may not be optimal in all cases, with ecological conditions sometimes favoring the persistence of decarcinization': Frog crabs show that in sediment environments, decarcinization can persist over millions of years rather than leading to extinction. This directly follows from the counterexample: the crab form is not always superior, and certain ecological niches can sustain decarcinized forms. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
